column1: Historical Timeline of Witch Hunts and Related Events

column6: Additional Context

column4: Pope Boniface VIII, Pope John XXII
column6: Early accusations against learned individuals and clergy; witchcraft seen as heresy and apostasy. Pope John XXII's bull "Super illius specula" (1326) condemned sorcery as heresy.

column4: Pope Clement V, King Philip IV of France
column6: Trials of the Knights Templar for heresy and devil worship, setting a precedent for later witch hunts. Many Templars were tortured and executed. The dissolution of the order allowed the French crown to seize their considerable assets.

column4: Alice Kyteler, Bishop Richard de Ledrede
column5: A Contemporary Narrative of the Proceedings Against Dame Alice Kyteler by Richard de Ledrede
column6: First recorded witch trial in Ireland. Kyteler escaped to England, but her maid Petronilla de Meath was flogged and burned at the stake, becoming the first person executed for witchcraft in Ireland.
Early systematic persecution of witches in Northern Italy, establishing the connection between witchcraft and diabolism.
First documented case of a "witches' sabbath" description in trial records.

column2: Trial and execution of Joan of Arc
column4: Joan of Arc, Pierre Cauchon, John of Lancaster (Duke of Bedford)
column5: The Trial of Jeanne d'Arc (trial records)
column6: Joan was accused of heresy and witchcraft. Her trial and execution had political motivations during the Hundred Years' War. She was rehabilitated and declared innocent in 1456, later canonized in 1920.
Early witch trials in Alpine regions that were described in Nider's influential work about witchcraft.
Influential early treatise describing witchcraft as a sect engaged in harmful magic and devil worship.

column4: Jacques du Boys, Pierre le Broussart
column6: The "Vauderie of Arras" trials where accusations of witchcraft were used against political enemies and wealthy citizens.
Encouraged the persecution of witches in the Rhine River valley, giving papal approval to Heinrich Kramer's witch-hunting activities and supporting the publication of Malleus Maleficarum.
Influential treatise that outlined methods for identifying and prosecuting witches, fueling witch hunts across Europe. Notably misogynistic, linking witchcraft primarily to women.

column2: Rise of witchcraft prosecutions in Switzerland
column6: Increasing number of witchcraft trials in Swiss territories, establishing patterns that would spread throughout Europe.
Imperial law code that codified witchcraft as a crime punishable by death throughout the Holy Roman Empire.
Made witchcraft a felony in England for the first time, though focused more on harmful magic than diabolism.

column4: Pope Paul III, Pope Julius III, Pope Pius IV
column6: Catholic Counter-Reformation council that reinforced Church authority and indirectly intensified witch-hunting by emphasizing rigid orthodoxy.
Made witchcraft a statutory crime in England, punishable by death, replacing Henry VIII's earlier law and expanding its scope.

column4: Catherine de' Medici, King Charles IX
column6: Mass killing of Huguenots (French Protestants) that increased religious tensions and suspicions throughout Europe, contributing to the climate of fear that enabled witch hunts.

column3: Western Germany, Low Countries, France, Northern Italy, Switzerland
column6: Mass executions; economic pressures, climate deterioration (Little Ice Age), religious warfare, and societal anxieties intensified witch hunts. Estimated 40,000-60,000 people executed for witchcraft during this period.

column2: Publication of The Discovery of Witchcraft
column6: One of the first books to criticize witch hunts and question the reality of witchcraft. King James I later ordered all copies burned.

column4: King James VI of Scotland (later James I of England), Agnes Sampson, Dr. John Fian
column6: Series of witch trials in Scotland triggered by storms that nearly sank King James's ship. King James's personal involvement led to his book Daemonologie, further fueling witch hunts. About 70-200 people were accused and many executed.

column4: Archbishop Johann VII von Schönenberg, Peter Binsfeld
column5: Tractatus de Confessionibus Maleficorum et Sagarum by Peter Binsfeld
column6: One of the largest witch trials in European history. Hundreds were executed, including many wealthy and prominent citizens. Entire villages were nearly depopulated.

column5: Tableau de l'inconstance des mauvais anges et démons by Pierre de Lancre
column6: Mass witch hunt in the Basque Country led by the French judge Pierre de Lancre. Approximately 7,000 cases investigated.
While not strictly a witch trial, Bruno was burned at the stake for heresy. His case exemplifies the period's religious intolerance and persecution of intellectual dissent.
James I's revision of the Elizabethan witchcraft law, making it more severe and explicitly connecting witchcraft with diabolism.

column5: Instructions by Alonso de Salazar Frías
column6: Spanish Inquisition investigation of alleged witch gatherings. Notable for Salazar's skeptical approach, which eventually limited Spanish witch hunts.

column4: Roger Nowell (Justice of the Peace), Thomas Potts
column5: The Wonderfull Discoverie of Witches in the Countie of Lancaster by Thomas Potts
column6: One of England's most famous witch trials, resulting in the execution of 10 people accused of witchcraft. Potts's detailed account became an influential text.
Mass witch hunt resulting in approximately 900 executions, including many children, clergy, and nobles.
Devastating religious war that created economic hardship and social instability, contributing to increased witch-hunting activity in affected regions.

column5: Of Plymouth Plantation by William Bradford
column6: Founding of the Plymouth Colony by English Separatists; early European settlement in North America. The colony brought European concepts of witchcraft to the New World.

column3: Prince-Bishopric of Bamberg, Holy Roman Empire
column4: Prince-Bishop Johann Georg II Fuchs von Dornheim
column6: One of the largest and most notorious witch hunts in Germany with approximately 1,000 executions, including the mayor, Johannes Junius, whose letter to his daughter describing his torture survives.
Establishment of Salem as an English settlement, which would later become infamous for its witch trials. Initially called Naumkeag, it was renamed Salem in 1629.

column3: Prince-Bishopric of Würzburg, Holy Roman Empire
column6: Second wave of trials resulting in approximately 160 more executions.

column5: The Devils of Loudun (1952) by Aldous Huxley
column6: Mass hysteria and accusations of demonic possession in a Ursuline convent, leading to the trial and execution of priest Urbain Grandier. The case had strong political motivations involving Cardinal Richelieu.

column2: Witchcraft made a felony in Massachusetts
column6: Legal framework established for prosecuting witchcraft in the American colonies, stating that "If any man or woman be a WITCH, that is, hath or consulteth with a familiar spirit, they shall be put to death."
Period of political instability and social upheaval that coincided with increased witch-hunting activity in England.

column5: The Discovery of Witches by Matthew Hopkins
column6: Most intense period of witch-hunting in English history. Hopkins and his associates were responsible for the deaths of 300-400 alleged witches in just three years.

column5: Instructions for Judges in Witchcraft Cases by Johan Rosenhane
column6: Beginning of intensive witch persecution in Sweden that would culminate in the "Great Noise" of 1668-1676.
Famous detailed voluntary confessions of witchcraft and devil worship that have influenced modern concepts of witchcraft. Gowdie's fate is unknown.
Major witch panic in Sweden resulting in approximately 280 executions and 300 formal accusations. Began with children's testimony and ended when a boy admitted to lying.
Last major Swedish witch trial resulting in 15 executions before the king ordered a halt to the trials.

column2: End of witch trials in France
column6: Royal edict decriminalizing witchcraft, though still punishing poisoners and fraudulent fortune-tellers.

column5: Memorable Providences, Relating to Witchcrafts and Possessions by Cotton Mather
column6: Four children exhibited strange behaviors attributed to witchcraft. Irish Catholic woman Goody Glover was executed. Cotton Mather's account of the case helped set the stage for the Salem witch trials.
Death of Rev. Samuel Parris's wife and mother of Betty Parris, contributing to household tensions that would play a role in the coming Salem witch trials.

column4: Samuel Parris, Cotton Mather, Samuel Sewall, William Stoughton, Betty Parris, Abigail Williams, Tituba
column5: The Wonders of the Invisible World by Cotton Mather, More Wonders of the Invisible World by Robert Calef
column6: Most notorious American witch paniOver 200 people were accused, 19 were hanged, 1 was pressed to death, and 5 died in prison. The trials ended when Governor Phips disbanded the special court and later issued pardons.

column2: Publication of More Wonders of the Invisible World
column5: More Wonders of the Invisible World
column6: Critical account of the Salem witch trials that challenged Cotton Mather's defense of the proceedings.
Official reversal of the attainders of those condemned during the Salem witch trials. Judge Samuel Sewall publicly apologized for his role in the trials.
Massachusetts legislature awarded compensation to the survivors and families of victims of the Salem witch trials.
Jane Wenham was convicted of witchcraft in 1712 but pardoned. Mary Hicks and her 9-year-old daughter were reportedly the last people executed for witchcraft in England in 1716.
Janet Horne was the last person legally executed for witchcraft in the British Isles.
Act repealed previous witchcraft statutes and replaced them with penalties for pretending to have magical powers. Effectively ended legal witch prosecutions in Britain.
Last known legal execution for witchcraft in Germany.
Often cited as the last execution for witchcraft in Europe, though some scholars dispute this was technically a witchcraft case. Göldi was accused of poisoning and using supernatural means.
Removed witchcraft from the list of criminal offenses in the Habsburg territories, part of wider Enlightenment-era legal reforms.

column5: Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen
column6: Political revolution that further marginalized belief in witchcraft among the educated classes and eliminated many ecclesiastical courts that had prosecuted witchcraft.
Period of mass executions during the French Revolution, sometimes compared to witch hunts for its targeting of "enemies of the state."

column2: Last execution for witchcraft in Spain
column6: A woman was reportedly burned at the stake for witchcraft in Seville. This was one of the last known executions for witchcraft in Western Europe.
Act granting Catholic civil rights in the UK, marking a decline in the religious persecution that had often accompanied witch hunts.
Modern spiritualist movement began, reclaiming some elements of folk beliefs related to communication with spirits.
Last recorded attempt at a witch trial in Germany, though the accused was not convicted.

column2: Publication of The Witchcraft Delusion in New England
column5: The Witchcraft Delusion in New England
column6: Important early historical study of the Salem witch trials.

column5: Various works including Magick in Theory and Practice
column6: Controversial occultist who contributed to modern reinterpretations of witchcraft and magic.

column2: Publication of Aradia, or the Gospel of the Witches
column5: Aradia, or the Gospel of the Witches
column6: Influential text claiming to preserve the religious beliefs of a surviving tradition of witches in Tuscany. Heavily influenced modern Wicca and neopaganism.
Period marked by political purges and show trials, often compared to witch hunts. During Stalin's Great Purge (1936-1938), over a million people were executed or sent to labor camps.

column2: Publication of The Witch-Cult in Western Europe
column6: Academic work proposing that European witch trials targeted practitioners of a pre-Christian fertility religion. Though now discredited, Murray's thesis heavily influenced modern Wicca.
Civil war accompanied by ideological purges comparable to witch hunts, resulting in tens of thousands of executions.
Mass political repression involving accusations of conspiracy and treason. Often compared to witch hunts due to forced confessions and the targeting of alleged hidden enemies.

Nazi persecution of Jews, Roma, homosexuals, and others using rhetoric of contamination and conspiracy similar to witch hunt discourse. The Holocaust resulted in approximately 6 million Jewish deaths and millions of other victims.

column4: Joseph McCarthy, House Un-American Activities Committee
column6: Period of intense fear of communist infiltration, leading to investigations and blacklisting of suspected communists. Commonly referred to as a "witch hunt" in modern discourse.

column2: Repeal of the Witchcraft Act in Britain
column6: Final repeal of the 1736 Witchcraft Act, replaced with laws targeting fraudulent claims to magical powers.
Book claiming to describe surviving witch traditions that became foundational to modern Wicca and the contemporary witchcraft revival.
Play using the Salem witch trials as an allegory for McCarthyism. Became one of the most influential artistic interpretations of the witch hunts.

column2: Growth of modern Wicca and neopaganism
column5: Various works including The Spiral Dance by Starhawk
column6: Period of significant growth for modern witchcraft as a religious movement, reclaiming and redefining the term "witch" in positive terms.

column5: Michelle Remembers by Michelle Smith and Lawrence Pazder
column6: Beginning of widespread fears about Satanic ritual abuse, often compared to historical witch hunts. Led to numerous false accusations and wrongful convictions.

column2: Modern witch hunts in Sub-Saharan Africa
column3: Democratic Republic of Congo, South Africa, Tanzania, Kenya, Nigeria, Ghana, Malawi, Central African Republic
column5: Academic studies including Witchcraft, Violence, and Democracy in South Africa by Adam Ashforth
column6: Ongoing incidents of witch-hunting in various African countries, resulting in thousands of deaths. Often targets elderly women, widows, and socially marginalized individuals. Complex interaction of traditional beliefs with modern economic and social pressures.
Moral panic involving accusations of Satanic ritual abuse in day cares and other institutions. Led to high-profile cases like the McMartin Preschool trial, with accusations eventually discredited.
Longest and most expensive criminal trial in American history at that time, involving accusations of ritual satanic abuse. All defendants were eventually acquitted.
Beginning of the end of communist regimes in Eastern Europe, which had conducted political purges often compared to witch hunts.
Transition from apartheid system, which had used accusations of communism and terrorism in ways comparable to witch hunts to suppress opposition.

column2: FBI report on Satanic Ritual Abuse
column5: Investigator's Guide to Allegations of Ritual Child Abuse
column6: Report finding no evidence of organized satanic abuse, helping to debunk the Satanic Panic.
Conflict accompanied by accusations of witchcraft and ritualistic violence.
Documentary highlighting the plight of women accused of witchcraft in Northern Ghana who live in "witch camps" for protection.
Mass killing of elderly people accused of witchcraft in western Kenya, highlighting the ongoing nature of witch persecution.

column5: Academic studies including The Invention of Child Witches in the Democratic Republic of Congo by Filip De Boeck
column6: Growing phenomenon of children being accused of witchcraft, often linked to Pentecostal and revivalist churches.
Repeal of colonial-era witchcraft law, though belief in witchcraft remains widespread.
Resolution condemning violence and discrimination against people accused of witchcraft.

column2: Mob kills woman accused of witchcraft
column6: Killing of woman accused of using sorcery in remote village, part of ongoing "sanguma" accusations in the country.

column3: Nigeria, Tanzania, India, Nepal, Papua New Guinea
column6: Continued reports of witch-hunting violence in multiple countries, with elderly women particularly vulnerable.

column5: Academic studies including COVID-19, Witchcraft Accusations and Health Rights Violations in South-Eastern Nigeria
column6: Pandemic linked to increased witchcraft accusations in some regions, with those accused of causing illness through supernatural means facing violence.

column5: Report on the Expert Workshop on Witchcraft and Human Rights
column6: International efforts to address ongoing witch hunts and develop practical solutions to protect vulnerable populations.
Strengthened legal protections for those accused of witchcraft, with increased penalties for making false accusations.
